topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of Dr. Thorne’s office gleamed under the soft lighting, a stark contrast to the memory of Mark’s sneering face in the schoolyard. Sarah shifted in her chair, the leather squeaking under her weight. She picked at a loose thread on her sleeve, her jaw tight. “So, you’re saying,” she began, her voice carefully level, “that my childhood tormentor is now helping people process trauma?” A faint tremor ran through her hands as she gripped the armrests. She watched the therapist lean forward, a practiced, gentle expression on his face. It felt like a deliberate provocation, this entire scenario. She wanted to push back, to expose the absurdity of it all, but the words caught in her throat, tasting like ash.

[story 2]

“Honestly, this whole thing is exhausting,” muttered David, running a hand through his already messy hair. He stared at the framed photo on the desk—a grainy picture of Mark, looking impossibly smug. David leaned back, the springs in his chair protesting loudly. He tapped his pen against his notepad with sharp, uneven movements. “I just don’t get it. He was the architect of all my awkward moments. Now he’s some esteemed professional. It just feels… manipulative.” He sighed, a deep, ragged sound. He pushed his coffee cup away, the ceramic clinking sharply against the wood. He needed to leave this room; the air in here felt too thick, too saccharine.

[story 3]

I watched him from across the room. Mark, impeccably dressed in a charcoal suit, was nodding sagely, his gaze warm and penetrating as he listened to a nervous client. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a familiar, unpleasant clenching sensation. I crossed my arms over my chest, feeling the tension radiating outward. *He*? The boy who used to trip me in the hallway? Now he held the reins of emotional understanding. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, the exhale barely audible. I focused intensely on the pattern of the wallpaper, refusing to let my eyes drift toward him, determined to maintain a façade of detached observation.
